meta.title: G32 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 6 Series G32 engine — from the 2.0 B48/B47 to the 3.0 B58/B57 six. Real UK costs, honest reliability data. Compare prices from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 6 Series G32 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned G32 engine costs between £2,800 and £8,000 supply-only, depending on the engine — the B47 diesel ranges £2,800–£5,000, the B48 petrol runs £3,200–£5,500, and the B58/B57 six-cylinder engines run £4,500–£8,000. Fitting adds 10–16 hours of labour at £65–£90/hr. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series G32 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58B30 3.0-litre six-cylinder (640i) is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern six-cylinder — proven durability, no major design flaws, and only minor HPFP and wastegate maintenance required. On diesel, the B57D30 (630d/640d) is also highly reliable, with timing chain issues described as 'exceedingly rare.'

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 6 Series G32?

acceptedAnswer.text: High-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure on the B58 petrol (640i), turbocharger wastegate rattle on the B58, EGR cooler failure on the B47 and B57 diesels (pre-recall units), and DPF blockage on diesels used predominantly for urban driving. The G32 also suffers from air suspension faults on higher-spec models.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 6 Series G32 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the G32's modular engines (B48, B58, B47, B57) are significantly more reliable than the N-series engines they replaced. The B58 is considered one of BMW's most reliable engines. For any G32, a thorough engine inspection and maintenance history check are essential, particularly on high-performance variants.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series G32 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Early B58 (2017–2019 640i) without documented HPFP or turbo maintenance carries some risk. Also avoid any B47/B57 diesel without documented EGR cooler recall completion. If you must have a B58, choose a 2020+ model with full service history and budget for HPFP inspection at 60,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my G32 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Long cranking or power loss (B58 HPFP), rattling noise at idle (B58 wastegate), coolant loss or overheating (B47/B57 EGR cooler), DPF warning lights on diesel, or persistent rough running on any high-mileage unit are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic tool for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 6 Series G32 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% BMW scrap threshold. Against a well-kept £20,000 G32, a £4,800 B58 recon is 24% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £12,000 early example, the same recon is 40% — borderline. For sub-£8,000 examples, we'd recommend walking away.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 6 Series G32 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 and B48 engines can comfortably exceed 150,000 miles with routine maintenance. The B57 diesel is equally durable, with 200,000+ miles achievable with proper servicing. The B47 diesel is also robust, provided the EGR cooler recall work is completed and the car is driven to allow DPF regeneration.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the G32 and other 6 Series generations?

acceptedAnswer.text: The G32 (2017–2023) is the second-generation BMW Gran Turismo and the first 6 Series Gran Turismo — it replaced the F07 5 Series Gran Turismo (2009–2017) and was not directly replaced in the 6 Series line-up. The G32 is based on the G30 5 Series and offers a raised seating position and a large liftback boot. Unlike the F13/F06 6 Series Coupé/Convertible (which were discontinued in 2018), the G32 is a five-door liftback executive car.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 6 Series G32 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate, vehicle documentation, and on a sticker under the bonnet. For 2.0 petrol, look for B48B20B (630i). For 3.0 petrol, look for B58B30A (pre-LCI 640i) or B58B30C (LCI 640i). For 2.0 diesel, look for B47D20B (620d). For 3.0 diesel, look for B57D30A (630d pre-LCI) or B57D30B (630d LCI, 640d). Tell us your variant and year, and we'll confirm.

hero.tagPill: BMW 6 Series G32 Gran Turismo • Liftback • 2017–2023
hero.h1: BMW 6 Series G32 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the G32-generation BMW 6 Series Gran Turismo — from the 2.0-litre B48 four-cylinder to the 3.0-litre B58 six and the B57 diesel range. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the B58's defining role in this generation's ownership story. Backed by real UK 6 Series enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 6 Series G32 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the G32 6 Series Gran Turismo specifically — from the 2017 launch through the 2020 LCI facelift and the introduction of 48-volt mild-hybrid technology.

overview.intro: The BMW G32 is the second generation of the BMW Gran Turismo range, a 5-door liftback executive car produced from June 2017 to August 2023. Built on the BMW CLAR platform and based on the G30 5 Series, the G32 succeeded the F07 5 Series Gran Turismo. The engine line-up at launch included the 630i (2.0-litre B48 four-cylinder), 640i (3.0-litre B58 six-cylinder), 630d (3.0-litre B57 six-cylinder diesel), and 640d xDrive (3.0-litre B57 diesel). A 620d (2.0-litre B47 four-cylinder diesel) was added in 2018. The 2020 LCI facelift introduced 48-volt mild-hybrid technology across the range. The G32 was discontinued in August 2023 with no direct successor.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW CLAR (Cluster Architecture) • Predecessor: BMW 5 Series Gran Turismo (F07) • Successor: None (discontinued August 2023) • Sibling body styles: None — G32 is sole Gran Turismo body style
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The G32 6 Series Gran Turismo is a niche model; enquiries are concentrated on the B58 six-cylinder petrol and B57 diesel engines, with values ranging from £15,000 for early 620d examples to £35,000+ for well-specced 640i/640d models.

items.engine: B58B30 3.0-litre six-cylinder (640i) — The 340 bhp B58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern six-cylinder. Delivering 450 Nm of torque and 0-62 in 5.3 seconds, it offers genuine performance with proven durability. Mild-hybrid versions (2020+) add efficiency without compromising reliability.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the sweet spot of performance, refinement, and reliability.

items.engine: B57D30 3.0-litre six-cylinder (630d/640d) — The 265–340 bhp B57 six-cylinder diesel delivers effortless torque (620–680 Nm), excellent refinement, and genuine durability. Timing chain issues are "exceedingly rare" compared to earlier BMW diesels.
items.whoItsFor: high-mileage buyers who want six-cylinder refinement without petrol running costs.

items.engine: B58B30 (early 640i) — While generally reliable, the B58 has documented high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failures and turbocharger wastegate rattle. HPFP failure causes long cranking, power loss, and misfires.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who accept the premium and keep the car under warranty.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre six-cylinder is the most expensive engine in the G32 line-up to replace, with reconditioned units costing £4,800–£8,000. HPFP replacement is a labour-intensive job — £800–£1,500 at a specialist — and turbocharger wastegate rattle can lead to full turbo replacement.

items.engine: B47D20 2.0-litre diesel (620d) — The 190 bhp B47 diesel offers the best balance of performance, economy, and running costs. It's the most economical G32 engine, parts are widely available, and the recon cost is the lowest in the range. Post-recall EGR cooler units are reliable.

B48B20 2.0-litre petrol (630i) — Often overshadowed by the six-cylinder 640i, the 258 bhp B48 delivers genuine performance — 0-62 in 6.3 seconds — with the economy of a four-cylinder and the proven reliability of BMW's modular engine family.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol refinement without the six-cylinder's fuel consumption.

economicsRule.title: The G32 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,800–£8,000 B58 recon against a £12,000–£18,000 early G32 sits at 27–67% of the car's value. On a sub-£12,000 example, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £20,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 24–40% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G32 Costs Least To Own?
Which G32 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: B58 640i — HPFP and turbo repairs add cost.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G32 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: B47 620d — the sweet spot between economy, performance, and running costs.

problems.title: B58 High-Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Failure
problems.rootCause: The high-pressure fuel pump on B58 engines can fail, leading to long cranking times before the engine starts, noticeable loss of power, engine misfires, and even stalling. The issue can be intermittent, making diagnosis challenging.
problems.recommendation: If your G32 640i exhibits long cranking or power loss, have the HPFP inspected immediately — an £800–£1,500 preventative repair is cheaper than the £4,800–£8,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare B58 engine prices for your G32 →

problems.title: B58 Turbocharger Wastegate Rattle
problems.rootCause: The wastegate components on B58 engines can wear over time, leading to a distinct rattling noise, especially noticeable at low RPMs or when the engine is idling. While it may not immediately affect performance, it can worsen and potentially lead to turbocharger failure.
problems.recommendation: On any G32 640i, listen for wastegate rattle at idle — an £800–£1,500 preventative repair is cheaper than the £2,500–£4,000 turbo replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare B58 turbo prices for your G32 →

problems.title: B47/B57 Diesel EGR Cooler & DPF Issues
problems.rootCause: The EGR cooler on B47 and B57 diesel engines can develop internal coolant leaks, leading to coolant loss. DPF blockage is also common on cars used predominantly for urban driving, with fault codes indicating DPF sensor failure.
problems.recommendation: On any G32 diesel, check the EGR cooler recall status immediately and ensure regular motorway runs for DPF regeneration — a £500–£1,200 preventative repair is cheaper than the £2,800–£5,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare B47/B57 engine prices for your G32 →
